summary: Jeffrey Wasserstrom, a historian of modern China, discusses the parallels and differences between Xi Jinping and Mao Zedong, exploring the historical and philosophical influences like Confucianism on contemporary China. The conversation delves into significant events such as the Tiananmen Square protests and the 'Tank Man,' examining China's sophisticated censorship methods and the complexities of the 'One Country, Two Systems' framework in Hong Kong, as well as the potential future of Taiwan amidst geopolitical tensions.

main_entities.name: Xi Jinping
main_entities.context: Current leader of China, compared to Mao Zedong, known for his personality cult, emphasis on order, and espousal of Confucian traditions.

main_entities.name: Mao Zedong
main_entities.context: Communist leader of China (1949-1976), subject of an intense personality cult, known for reveling in chaos and being anti-Confucian.

main_entities.name: Confucius
main_entities.context: Ancient Chinese philosopher whose ideas of stable hierarchies, mutual obligations, and meritocracy are celebrated by Xi Jinping, contrasting with Mao's views.

main_entities.name: Tiananmen Square Protests (1989)
main_entities.context: Massive student-led movement in China, initially aimed at reforming the Communist Party and addressing corruption, brutally suppressed by the military.

main_entities.name: Tank Man
main_entities.context: Anonymous individual who bravely stood in front of a line of tanks near Tiananmen Square, an iconic image banned in China, symbolizing resistance.

main_entities.name: Censorship in China
main_entities.context: Described using Margaret Roberts' 'fear, friction, and flooding' model, highlighting its selective, subtle, and pervasive nature, particularly concerning direct criticism of leaders or sensitive events.

main_entities.name: One Country, Two Systems
main_entities.context: The framework for Hong Kong's autonomy after its 1997 handover, intended as a model for Taiwan, but seen as eroding over time, leading to protests.

main_entities.name: Hong Kong Protests (2019)
main_entities.context: Large-scale demonstrations against mainlandization and an extradition law, seen as a defense of Hong Kong's rule of law and autonomy, drawing millions of participants.

main_entities.name: Taiwan
main_entities.context: Island nation viewed by Beijing as a crucial part of China's 'rejuvenation' and a potential flashpoint for military conflict, its fate influenced by Hong Kong's experience.

main_entities.name: Great Leap Forward
main_entities.context: Mao Zedong's disastrous economic and social campaign (1958-1962) that led to a catastrophic famine and the deaths of 30-45 million people.

main_entities.name: Chinese Communist Party (CCP)
main_entities.context: The ruling party of China, its history, internal dynamics, and methods of maintaining power, including its nationalistic narratives.

main_entities.name: Korean War
main_entities.context: Conflict (1950-1953) in which China intervened, portrayed by Mao and the CCP as a triumph against Western powers, a narrative still used today.

main_entities.name: Deng Xiaoping
main_entities.context: Paramount leader after Mao, initiated economic reforms, and oversaw the 'One Country, Two Systems' deal for Hong Kong, leading to decades of economic growth.

key_details.fact: Xi Jinping is the first leader since Mao Zedong to have a sustained personality cult, with his books and speeches prominently displayed.
Mao Zedong reveled in chaos and disorder, drawing inspiration from the Monkey King, a stark contrast to Xi Jinping's emphasis on stability and order.
Confucianism is based on stable, hierarchical relationships (e.g., father-son, husband-wife, emperor-minister) where both sides have mutual obligations, not egalitarianism.

The Tiananmen Square protests in 1989 were primarily an effort to push the Chinese Communist Party to live up to its stated ideals, combat corruption, and support liberalization, rather than to overthrow communism.
The identity and fate of the 'Tank Man' remain unknown, with the image of him standing before tanks being banned within China.

Chinese censorship operates through 'fear, friction, and flooding,' making information difficult to access, filling media with preferred narratives, and selectively punishing high-profile individuals.
The 'One Country, Two Systems' agreement for Hong Kong in 1984 was also presented as a potential model for Taiwan's absorption into the PRC.
The Great Leap Forward (1958-1962) resulted in 30-45 million deaths, largely due to famine exacerbated by officials' fear of reporting bad news to Mao.
Mao Zedong was posthumously assessed by the CCP as '70% right, 30% wrong,' a similar assessment Mao himself made of Stalin.

topics: Geopolitics in East and Southeast Asia

other_persons_mentioned.name: Lex Fridman
other_persons_mentioned.context: Podcast host and interviewer.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Hu Jintao
other_persons_mentioned.context: Former leader of China, mentioned in context of mixing historical elements.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Socrates
other_persons_mentioned.context: Ancient Greek philosopher, mentioned as a contemporary of Confucius in the Axial Age.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Mencius
other_persons_mentioned.context: Early interpreter of Confucius, discussed for his idea of heaven's mandate justifying rebellion.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Stalin
other_persons_mentioned.context: Soviet leader, compared to Mao regarding contradictions and Mao's 70/30 assessment.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Fang Lizhi
other_persons_mentioned.context: Chinese physicist and liberalizing intellectual, inspired 1986 student protests.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Jan and Dean
other_persons_mentioned.context: American surf rock band, their 1986 Shanghai concert symbolized limited openness for students.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Hu Yaobang
other_persons_mentioned.context: Reformist leader within the CCP, his unexpected death in 1989 triggered the Tiananmen protests.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Cui Jian
other_persons_mentioned.context: Popular Chinese rock musician, performed at Tiananmen Square protests.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Margaret Roberts
other_persons_mentioned.context: Author of a book on Chinese censorship, her 'fear, friction, flooding' model is discussed.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Aldous Huxley
other_persons_mentioned.context: Author of 'Brave New World', discussed in comparison to Orwell and his views on future societal control.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Eric Blair (George Orwell)
other_persons_mentioned.context: Author of '1984' and 'Animal Farm', discussed in comparison to Huxley and his analysis of language in totalitarian regimes.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Margaret Atwood
other_persons_mentioned.context: Author of 'Handmaid's Tale', mentioned as a fan of both '1984' and 'Brave New World'.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Zamyatin
other_persons_mentioned.context: Author of 'We', mentioned as an inspiration for both Orwell and Huxley.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Christina Larson
other_persons_mentioned.context: Journalist, discussed for her insight into China having both the 'best and worst internet experience'.

other_persons_mentioned.name: William Gibson
other_persons_mentioned.context: Author of 'Neuromancer', referred to Singapore as 'Disneyland with the death penalty'.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Emily Fang
other_persons_mentioned.context: NPR journalist and author of 'Let Only Red Flowers Bloom', discussed for her insights on Xi Jinping's narrowing of societal spaces.

other_persons_mentioned.name: James Palmer
other_persons_mentioned.context: Journalist and editor at Foreign Policy, wrote about the opaque nature of Chinese elite politics.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Putin
other_persons_mentioned.context: Russian President, mentioned in context of Xi Jinping's shared distaste for Gorbachev's perceived weakness.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Gorbachev
other_persons_mentioned.context: Last leader of the Soviet Union, his policies and the collapse of the Soviet Bloc are contrasted with China's post-Tiananmen path.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Chiang Kai-shek
other_persons_mentioned.context: Leader of the Nationalist Party (KMT), Mao's great rival, retreated to Taiwan, and had a personality cult, celebrating Confucianism.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Melissa Chan
other_persons_mentioned.context: Journalist for Al Jazeera, an early example of a person expelled from China for reporting on Xinjiang.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Gui Minhai
other_persons_mentioned.context: Hong Kong bookseller and Swedish citizen, kidnapped and imprisoned in mainland China for publishing sensitive books about CCP leaders.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Donald Trump
other_persons_mentioned.context: Former US President, discussed regarding his relationship with Xi Jinping, the trade war, and his unpredictable nature.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Barbara Walters
other_persons_mentioned.context: Journalist, interviewed former Chinese leader Jiang Zemin.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Jiang Zemin
other_persons_mentioned.context: Former paramount leader of China, known for being interviewed by Barbara Walters and showing some cosmopolitanism.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Ernest Hemingway
other_persons_mentioned.context: American author, mentioned as someone Xi Jinping might genuinely admire, fitting a 'macho' image.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Victor Hugo
other_persons_mentioned.context: French author, admired by Xi Jinping, but his work 'Les Misérables' became a protest song in Hong Kong, and he criticized European destruction of Beijing's Old Summer Palace.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Mark Twain
other_persons_mentioned.context: American author, had a good reputation in China due to his criticism of American imperialism.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Han Han
other_persons_mentioned.context: Chinese race car driver, filmmaker, and blogger, discussed for his humorous take on US-China economic interdependence.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Sun Yat-sen
other_persons_mentioned.context: Provisional president of the Republic of China and founder of the Nationalist Party, initially allied with the Communist Party.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Lenin
other_persons_mentioned.context: Russian revolutionary, whose ideas of anti-imperialism and a vanguard party were attractive to early Chinese communists.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Khrushchev
other_persons_mentioned.context: Soviet leader after Stalin, his de-Stalinization efforts haunted Mao and contributed to the Sino-Soviet split.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Václav Havel
other_persons_mentioned.context: Czech playwright and dissident who became president, an inspirational figure for activists, mentioned for his unexpected political success.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Rebecca Solnit
other_persons_mentioned.context: Author of 'No Straight Road Takes You There', discussed for her perspective on the long-term, often non-linear nature of social struggles.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Miklós Haraszti
other_persons_mentioned.context: Hungarian friend of Wasserstrom, an activist before 1989, who witnessed the end of communist rule but later became a critic of tightening controls under Orbán.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Geremie Barmé
other_persons_mentioned.context: New Zealand-based China specialist, who discusses the concept of 'the other China' – a diverse and cosmopolitan Chinese identity.
